1: Psalm 10 continues the acrostic of Psalm 9 in the Hebrew Psalter — this is the second panel of the A-to-Z meditation on divine justice. The opening lāmmāh (why) is the classic challenge of the lament genre — a direct rhetorical confrontation with God's apparent absence. "Hide yourself in times of trouble (ʿittôt bĕṣārāh)" uses the idiom of God hiding his face (hissatēr pānîm), which in covenant theology signals withdrawal of favor or presence (Isaiah 8:17; Psalms 54:8; Psalms 13:1; Psalms 27:9). This psalm is one of the most vivid portraits of social oppression in the Psalter.
2: "In his arrogance (bĕgaʾăwat) the wicked person persecutes the poor" — the predatory imagery escalates through the psalm: the wicked is described as a prowling hunter (Psalms 10:8-10) and a crouching lion (Psalms 10:9). This social portrait matches realities well-documented in ancient Israel and Second Temple Judaism: wealthy landowners exploiting tenant farmers, creditors seizing collateral in violation of Torah protections (cf. Nehemiah 5:1-5; Amos 8:4-6; Isaiah 5:8). The retribution petition — "let him be caught in the very schemes he has devised" — is the boomerang theme.
3: "The wicked person boasts of the cravings of his heart (taʾăwat napšô)" — self-satisfaction in greed. "Curses and spurns the LORD" — the Hebrew uses the verb bēraḵ (bless) as a euphemism for "curse" (the same softening appears in Job 1:5, 11; Psalms 2:5, 9), a scribal device to avoid writing the blaspheming word directly. The contrast throughout the psalm is between the wicked's self-blessing and YHWH's blessing of the poor (Psalms 9:18).
4: "In all his thinking, there is no place for God" — the classic statement of practical atheism. ʾên ʾĕlōhîm kol-maḥšĕbôtāyw (literally: all his thoughts — "no God"). This is not the philosophical atheism of the Enlightenment but the functional atheism of one who acts as if God does not govern human affairs. Second Temple wisdom literature (Ben Sira, Wisdom of Solomon) treats this as the root of all wickedness: the failure to account for divine judgment in one's practical decisions.
5: "His ways always succeed" — the theological scandal at the heart of this lament: the wicked prosper. This is the pressing question of Pss 37, 49, 73, and the book of Job. "Your judgments are too high for him to perceive" — not that divine judgments are intellectually incomprehensible, but that the wicked's diminished moral vision cannot see them. The wicked "sneers at all his enemies" — success breeds contempt for any opposition. The Psalter does not deny this experience; it holds the tension honestly.
6: "He says in his heart, 'I will never be shaken (bal-ʾemmoṭ); through all generations I will face no trouble.'" The heart-speech of the wicked (Psalms 10:6, 11, 13) is a rhetorical device to expose the interior logic of oppression. The phrase bal-ʾemmoṭ ("will not be shaken") deliberately inverts the security language the Psalter applies to the righteous (Psalms 15:5; Psalms 16:8; Psalms 21:7; Psalms 62:2). The wicked has appropriated covenant-stability language for his own arrogance — a theological usurpation the psalmist exposes.
7: "His mouth is full of cursing, deceit, and fraud; under his tongue are trouble and wickedness" — the anatomy of wicked speech. In ANE thought, curse formulas carried real causal power; to be under a curse was a genuine social-spiritual threat. The under-the-tongue image is forensic: the tongue conceals what the mouth will eventually release. "Trouble (ʿāmāl) and wickedness (ʾāwen)" — ʾāwen is a key word in the Psalter's moral vocabulary, combining meanings of wickedness, idolatry, and emptiness.
8: "He lurks in ambush near the villages" — the rural social setting: isolated farmsteads and small communities were vulnerable to strongmen who operated outside the protection of urban courts. The "innocent" (nāqî) he kills are those without legal standing. Vv. 8-10 use predator-prey imagery (lion, net, snare) that was standard in Egyptian and Mesopotamian wisdom texts for describing the oppressor. The psalmist maps ANE hunting vocabulary onto social exploitation — making the oppressor's predatory nature visible.
9: "He crouches like a lion in his lair; he lies in wait to catch the poor" — the lion as predator is one of the Psalter's persistent metaphors (Psalms 7:2; Psalms 17:12; Psalms 22:13; Psalms 57:4). In Israelite pastoral culture, lions preying on livestock were a real threat (1 Samuel 17:34-37; Amos 3:12); the metaphor carries immediate visceral force for an agricultural audience. The poor (ʿānî) are structurally vulnerable: without land, family network, or access to courts — those who cannot defend themselves.
10: "He crouches and springs, and the helpless fall by his strength" — dakkîm (the crushed/helpless) echoes the dak of Psalms 9:9. The abruptness of the verse (only 3 words in Hebrew: "he crouches, he springs, the weak fall") mimics the sudden violence of the predatory act. In the ancient economy, "falling by strength" could mean debt-bondage, seizure of property, or physical coercion — all forms of power available to the strong against those without social protection.
11: "God has forgotten; he has hidden his face; he will never notice" — the wicked's three-part denial mirrors Psalms 10:4 and 6: a consistent alternative theology. "Hiding the face" (hissatîr pānîm) was a covenant idiom for divine withdrawal that the wicked here declares permanent. The theological scandal of divine silence in the face of oppression was not only a personal spiritual crisis but a communal challenge to the entire covenant framework in which YHWH protects the vulnerable.
12: "Rise up, O LORD! O God, lift up your hand! Do not forget the afflicted!" — the imperative petitions (qûmāh, nĕśāʾ yādĕkā) directly counter the wicked's claim that God has forgotten. "Lift up your hand" is the gesture of power and striking — the raised hand prepares to act (cf. Exodus 7:5; Deuteronomy 32:40). This petition echoes Psalms 9:19 and reflects the liturgical context: the congregation physically enacting the prayer, perhaps with raised hands, prostration, or crying out.
13: "Why does the wicked person despise God and say in his heart, 'You won't call me to account'?" — nāʾaṣ (despise, treat with contempt) is a strong term for active repudiation. The wicked's inner speech "You won't call me to account" (lōʾ tidrōš) uses the verb dāraš (seek/require/demand): God will neither inquire into his actions nor demand accountability. This inverts the covenant: in which YHWH does seek and does require (Deuteronomy 18:19; Amos 5:4; Isaiah 55:6).
14: "But you do see! You observe trouble and grief and take it into your hands" — the rhetorical pivot. Against the wicked's assumption of divine indifference, the psalmist asserts divine attentiveness: God sees, observes, and intervenes. "The helpless person (yātôm — the fatherless/orphan) entrusts himself to you" — the orphan was among the most legally vulnerable in ancient Israelite society. To be fatherless was to lose one's gōʾēl (kinsman-redeemer), one's advocate before courts, and one's social standing.
15: "Break the arm of the wicked and the evildoer" — "arm" (zĕrôaʿ) is the standard symbol of power and strength (cf. Ezekiel 30:21-22, where God explicitly breaks Pharaoh's arm). To break the arm is to permanently incapacitate the means of oppression. "Call his wickedness to account until there is none left" — the demand is not merely personal punishment but the exhaustion of the capacity for wickedness: complete moral accountability leading to complete moral incapacitation.
16: "The LORD is King for ever and ever; the nations will vanish from his land" — the divine kingship proclamation (yhwh melek ʿôlām wāʿed) closes the lament with the enthronement affirmation. "The nations will vanish from his land (ʾarṣô)" — the land belongs to YHWH as its true owner; those who occupy it without acknowledging him are transient tenants. In Second Temple theology this pointed both to Eretz Israel and, eschatologically, to the whole earth over which YHWH would rule.
17: "LORD, you have heard the desire (taʾăwat) of the humble" — this word (taʾăwāh) was used in Psalms 10:3 for the wicked's cravings. Now it reappears for the humble's longing — a deliberate contrast. The wicked's desires lead to exploitation; the humble's desire is for vindication and survival. "You will strengthen their heart" — in Hebrew thought the heart (lēb) is the seat of will, intention, and courage; to strengthen the heart is to resolve and empower the will for perseverance.
18: "To vindicate the fatherless and the oppressed, so that mortal man will no longer strike terror" — the psalm closes where it opened: the question of human power over the vulnerable. The final petition is cosmic in scope: that the whole pattern of oppression over the defenseless would end. "Mortal man" (ʾĕnôš min-hāʾāreṣ — man from the earth) reprises Psalms 9:20's deflation of human arrogance. The Pss 9-10 acrostic closes with this eschatological hope: the human terror-imposer brought to nothing before the eternal King.
